tiles.name: Mercado 28
tiles.funFact: Downtown Cancún's most famous market where locals and tourists haggle for silver jewelry, hammocks, and Mexican handicrafts since 1973.

tiles.name: Parque de las Palapas
tiles.funFact: The cultural heart of downtown Cancún where families gather every evening for free concerts, street food, and traditional dance performances.

tiles.name: Tren Maya - Estación Cancún
tiles.funFact: The Tren Maya's Cancún station connects the resort city to the entire Yucatán Peninsula across 1,554 km of track through five Mexican states.

tiles.name: Hotel Zone North
tiles.funFact: The northern section of Cancún's famous L-shaped sandbar features calm bay-side waters perfect for families and over 20 major resort properties.

tiles.name: Hotel Zone South
tiles.funFact: The southern stretch faces the open Caribbean with powerful surf, and is home to Cancún's most exclusive all-inclusive mega-resorts.

tiles.name: Playa Delfines
tiles.funFact: One of Cancún's only public beaches with no hotel frontage, famous for its giant colorful "CANCÚN" sign and spectacular turquoise waters.

tiles.name: Playa Forum
tiles.funFact: Located at the bend of the Hotel Zone, this beach sits next to Cancún's famous party district and offers some of the calmest swimming waters.

tiles.name: CFE Cancún
tiles.funFact: CFE powers Cancún's massive tourism infrastructure, supplying electricity to over 30,000 hotel rooms and the city's 900,000 residents.

tiles.name: Punta Cancún
tiles.funFact: The elbow-shaped tip of the Hotel Zone where the Caribbean Sea meets the Nichupté Lagoon, home to the densest concentration of nightclubs and restaurants.

tiles.name: Punta Nizuc
tiles.funFact: The southern tip of the Hotel Zone is home to MUSA, an underwater museum with over 500 life-sized sculptures submerged in the crystal-clear reef.

tiles.name: Tren Maya - Estación Puerto Morelos
tiles.funFact: This station serves the quiet fishing village of Puerto Morelos, gateway to the Mesoamerican Barrier Reef, the second largest in the world.

tiles.name: El Rey Ruins
tiles.funFact: Ancient Maya ruins right in the Hotel Zone dating to 300 BC, where hundreds of wild iguanas freely roam among 47 archaeological structures.

tiles.name: Isla Mujeres
tiles.funFact: The "Island of Women" was sacred to the Maya goddess Ixchel. Just 13 km offshore, it features Punta Sur, the easternmost point of Mexico.

tiles.name: Puerto Morelos
tiles.funFact: A charming fishing village with a famously leaning lighthouse damaged by Hurricane Beulah in 1967, now a national marine park protecting pristine reefs.

tiles.name: Playa del Carmen
tiles.funFact: Once a tiny ferry port to Cozumel, it became one of the fastest-growing cities in the world. Its Quinta Avenida stretches 20 blocks of shops and restaurants.

tiles.name: Cozumel
tiles.funFact: Jacques Cousteau made this island world-famous in 1961 for its crystal-clear reefs. It is now the busiest cruise port in Mexico with over 1,200 ships annually.

tiles.name: Xcaret
tiles.funFact: A massive eco-archaeological park built around ancient Maya ruins and underground rivers, hosting a nightly spectacular with 300 performers celebrating Mexican culture.

tiles.name: Tren Maya - Estación Playa del Carmen
tiles.funFact: This station links Playa del Carmen to the Tren Maya network, allowing visitors to reach Chichén Itzá and Mérida without driving.

tiles.name: Xel-Há
tiles.funFact: The world's largest natural aquarium where freshwater cenotes meet the Caribbean Sea, creating a snorkeling paradise with over 90 marine species.

tiles.name: Tulum Ruins
tiles.funFact: The only major Maya city built on a Caribbean cliff, Tulum was a thriving trading port from 1200 to 1521 AD and is Mexico's third most visited archaeological site.

tiles.name: CAPA
tiles.funFact: CAPA manages Cancún's water supply sourced from underground cenotes and aquifers in the porous limestone of the Yucatán Peninsula.

tiles.name: Cenote Ik Kil
tiles.funFact: A breathtaking 60-meter-deep sinkhole near Chichén Itzá with vines cascading from the rim. The Maya considered cenotes sacred portals to the underworld.

tiles.name: Zona Hotelera
tiles.funFact: Cancún's iconic 23 km L-shaped barrier island was planned from scratch in 1970 by a government computer program that selected it as the ideal resort location.

tiles.name: La Isla Shopping
tiles.funFact: An open-air shopping village built over the Nichupté Lagoon with canals running through it, featuring an interactive aquarium and sunset waterfront dining.

tiles.name: Kukulcán Boulevard
tiles.funFact: Named after the feathered serpent god, this 26 km road is the main artery of the Hotel Zone, connecting all major resorts, malls, and nightlife venues.

tiles.name: Tren Maya - Estación Tulum
tiles.funFact: The Tulum station serves as a gateway to the Riviera Maya's southern coast and provides direct rail access to the ancient ruins of the Yucatán interior.

tiles.name: Nichupté Lagoon
tiles.funFact: This 3,000-hectare lagoon system separates the Hotel Zone from the mainland, home to crocodiles, manatees, and mangrove forests vital to Cancún's ecosystem.

tiles.name: Chichén Itzá
tiles.funFact: One of the New Seven Wonders of the World, the Kukulcán pyramid creates a shadow serpent effect during equinoxes that draws over 2.6 million visitors annually.
